subnotes/apostrophe.html.title: Apostrophe
subnotes/apostrophe.html.description:
Apostrophe is a figure of speech in which a speaker directly addresses an absent person, a deceased individual, an abstract concept, or an inanimate object as if it were present, alive, and capable of responding.
呼告是一种修辞手法，指说话者直接对不在场的人、已故者、抽象概念或无生命的物体进行称呼，仿佛对方就在眼前、具有生命且能够回应。

subnotes/composed-by-the-sea-side-near-c.title: Composed by the Sea-Side near Calais
subnotes/composed-by-the-sea-side-near-c.description: William Wordsworth Fair Star of evening, Splendour of the west,Star of my Country!--on the horizon’s brinkThou hangest, stooping, as might seem, to sinkOn England’s bosom; yet well pleased to rest,Meanwhile, and be to her a glorious crestConspicuous to the Nations. Thou, I think,Should’st be my Country’s emblem; and should’st wink,Bright Star! with laughter on her banners, drestIn thy fresh beauty. There! that dusky spotBeneath thee, that is England; there she lies.Blessings be on you both! one hope, one lot,One life, one glory!--I, with many a fearFor my dear Country, many heartfelt sighs,Among men who do not love her, linger here.it's a Sonnet, Petrarchan sonnet The Peace of Amiens was a treaty signed on March 25,1802 between France and Britain; but this peace are temporary, soon Britain started the war again.
subnotes/composed-by-the-sea-side-near-c.sourcePath: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/Composed by the Sea-Side near Calais.md

subnotes/i-wandered-lonely-as-a-cloud.ht.title: I  Wandered Lonely as a Cloud
subnotes/i-wandered-lonely-as-a-cloud.ht.description: a lyric poem focusing on the poet's response to the beauty of nature four six-line stanzas following a quatrain-couplet rhyme scheme: ababcc. with each line metered in iambic tetrameter Wordsworth, his sister and his wife were come together to see the daffodilsWilliam WordsworthI wandered lonely as a Cloud That floats on high o'er Vales and Hills, When all at once I saw a crowd, A host of golden Daffodils; Beside the Lake, beneath the trees, Fluttering and dancing in the breeze.Continuous as the stars that shine And twinkle on the Milky Way, They stretched in never-ending line Along the margin of a bay: Ten thousand saw I at a glance, Tossing their heads in sprightly dance.The waves beside them danced, but they Out-did the sparkling waves in glee: A Poet could not but be gay In such a jocund company: I gazed—and gazed—but little thought What wealth the shew to me had brought:For oft when on my couch I lie In vacant or in pensive mood, They flash upon that inward eye Which is the bliss of solitude, And then my heart with pleasure fills, And dances with the Daffodils
subnotes/i-wandered-lonely-as-a-cloud.ht.sourcePath: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/I  Wandered Lonely as a Cloud.md

subnotes/john-keats.html.title: John Keats
subnotes/john-keats.html.description:
under the guardianship of two London merchants appointed by his maternal grandmother after his mother’s death. withdrawn from the Clarke’s School at age of 15 by his guardian to apprentice with an apothecary-surgeon and study medicine in a London hospital. became a licensed apothecary(药剂师) in 1816, but never practiced this profession, deciding instead to write poetry. Met Leigh Hunt around 1816, an influential editor of the Examiner, who published his sonnets "On First Looking into Chapman's Homer" and "O Solitude." Published his first volume, Poems by John Keats, in 1817.(Shelley fond Keats and advised him to polish his poem but Keats didn't follow) Published Endymion, a four-thousand-line erotic/allegorical romance in 1818. contracted tuberculosis in the autumn of 1819. published his third and best volume of poetry, Lamia, Isabella, The Eve of St. Agnes, and Other Poems in July 1820, which also contains the unfinished Hyperion, and three poems considered among the finest in the English language: Ode on a Grecian Urn, Ode on Melancholy, and Ode to Nightingale. died in Rome on February 23, 1821, at the age of twenty-five, and was buried in the Protestant cemetery last request: to be buried under an unnamed tombstone, with words "Here lies one whose name was written in water" a theory developed by Keats the ability to exist in the condition of uncertainties, miseries, doubts etc searching for possibility that by uniting Truth and Beauty in a single sublime experience. Art possesses the power to overcome the world of pain and death, and to redeem man’s doubts and uncertainties through a brief spiritual transcendence. A famous theory of poetry What the imagination seizes as Beauty must be truth The message conveyed in his poetry therefore is the lasting power of beauty and its union with truth a meditation on the immortal beauty of the nightingale's song and the sadness of the poet who actually responds to the beauty of the bird's song with both happiness and ache. Ode on a Grecian Urn (5) O Attic shape! Fair attitude! with brede Of marble men and maidens overwrought, With forest branches and the trodden weed; Thou, silent form, dost tease us out of thought As doth eternity: Cold Pastoral! When old age shall this generation waste, Thou shalt remain, in midst of other woe Than ours, a friend to man, to whom thou say'st, "Beauty is truth, truth beauty,"—that is all Ye know on earth, and all ye need to know. The Greek power of art lie in capturing beauty of nature and On First Looking into Chapman's Homer
Bright Star

subnotes/john-milton.html.title: John Milton
subnotes/john-milton.html.description:
One of the greatest and most influential poets, ranking with Chaucer and Shakespeare. writing both poetry and prose best know works Paradise Lost known as "the lady of Christ's" because of Christ's College, Cambrige University who wrote 23 sonnets and 1-6 and 23 about love; 15-18 about public policy; 8,11,12 about peace and freedom; 9,10, 14 addressing female friends and 13,20,21 addressing male friends, 7,19,22 about his will to serve God. but Milton is a quite religious person so every his works contains religious belief
Blank verseMiltonic sonnetKeeps the Petrarchan length and rhyming scheme but don't have the stanza break between the octave and the sestet

subnotes/sonnet-1-by-milton.html.title: sonnet 1 by Milton
subnotes/sonnet-1-by-milton.html.description:
John MiltonO nightingale, that on yon bloomy spray
Warbl'st at eve, when all the woods are still,
Thou with fresh hope the lover's heart dost fill,
While the jolly hours lead on propitious May,Thy liquid notes that close the eye of Day,
First heard before the shallow cuckoo's bill
Portend success in love; O if Jove's will
Have linkt that amorous power to thy soft lay,Now timely sing, ere the rude Bird of Hate
Foretell my hopeless doom, in some Grove nigh:
As thou from year to year hast sung too late
For my relief; yet hadst no reason why,Whether the Muse, or Love call thee his mate,
Both them I serve, and of their train am I.
subnotes/sonnet-1-by-milton.html.sourcePath: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/sonnet 1 by Milton.md

subnotes/sonnet-18.html.title: sonnet 18
subnotes/sonnet-18.html.description: Shall I compare thee to a summer’s day? (similie)Thou art more lovely and more temperate: Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May, (personification: summer->tenant)And summer’s lease hath all too short a date; Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines, (metaphor: the eye of heaven->sun)And often is his gold complexion dimm'd; (complextion: 面色，脸色) (personification: his gold complextion dimm'd->sunshine is too weak)And every fair from fair sometime declines, (alliteration: fair from fair; chance or nature's changing course) (the first fair refers to beauty concept itself and the second fair refers to person)By chance or nature’s changing course untrimm'd; (untrimmed未加修饰的)But thy eternal summer shall not fade, (metaphor: eternal summer -> eternal beauty) Nor lose possession of that fair thou ow’st; Nor shall death brag thou wander’st in his shade, (brag: 吹嘘)When in eternal lines to time thou grow’st: keep your beauty in peom will be as eternal as time the theme is language, poem will be eternal So long as men can breathe or eyes can see,    So long lives this, and this gives life to thee. So long as -> as long as So long live this -> so this will live eternal收录于1609年出版的《莎士比亚十四行诗》，共154首；本诗属Fair Youth系列（1–126首），献给一位神秘贵族青年结构：3个四行联（quatrains）+ 1个双行对句（couplet），共14行 韵律：ABAB CDCD EFEF GG 节奏：抑扬格五音步 Iambic pentameterThe four steps (structure) of Shakespeare sonnet Introduction (opening) Elucidation of the theme (developing; raising problems) Transition to another viewpoint (changing) Summing uo (concluding and may reinforce the theme; give a solution) 类似中国的 起承转合 Because of geography, in Britain spring still really cold, but summer is mild, so compare some on to summer in Britain is the highest praise Shakespeare even elevate it higher, Thou art more lovely and more temperate; summer is good but still transient but you are better and eternal Introduction (opening) you are so beautiful Elucidation of the theme (developing; raising problems) but every beautiful things will fade eventually Transition to another viewpoint (changing) but your beauty will not fade Summing uo (concluding and may reinforce the theme; give a solution) because language and poem will eternal exist and you will be in this poem the same theme poems sonnet55 sonnet60

headers.heading: The four steps (structure) of Shakespeare sonnet
why compare his beloved one to summer's day

subnotes/sonnet-19-by-milton.html.title: sonnet 19 by Milton
subnotes/sonnet-19-by-milton.html.description:
John MiltonWhen I consider how my light is spent,
Ere half my days in this dark world and wide,
And that one talent which is death to hide
Lodged with me useless, though my soul more bentTo serve therewith my Maker, and present
My true account, lest He returning chide,
'Doth God exact day-labour, light denied?'
I fondly ask. But Patience, to preventThat murmur, soon replies: 'God doth not need
Either man's work or his own gifts. Who best
Bear his mild yoke, they serve him best. His stateIs kingly: thousands at his bidding speed,
And post o'er land and ocean without rest;
They also serve who only stand and wait.' On His Blindness - Rearranged ( the first 8 lines): I fondly ask, "Doth God exact day-labor, light denied?" when I consider (1) how my light is spent ere half my days in this dark world and wide and (2) (how) that one talent which is death to hide lodged with me useless(ly) though my soul (be) more bent to serve therewith my Maker, and (to) present my true account, lest He ( i.e. my maker) returning (should) chide (me)...# On His Blindness - Rearranged ( the first 8 lines): I fondly ask, "Doth God exact day-labor, light denied?" when I consider (1) how my light is spent ere half my days in this dark world and wide and (2) (how) that one talent which is death to hide lodged with me useless(ly) though my soul (be) more bent to serve therewith my Maker, and (to) present my true account, lest He ( i.e. my maker) returning (should) chide (me)... Bear his mild yoke, they serve him best. His state Is kingly: thousands at his bidding speed, And post o'er land and ocean without rest; They also serve who only stand and wait.'
=> you can serve god more than one way
=> so although Milton is blind, he still believe god and will continue to serve the god (physically can't but mentally can) We can learn from this poem: regardless your output, pay attention to your attitude, you should be active and do things
God requires service.
The poet is blind, but the poem does not deal with his physical handicap, but his spiritual one.
The solutions that the poem offers are spiritual.

headers.heading: The experience communicated in the poem is universal

subnotes/sonnet-19-by-milton.html.sourcePath: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/sonnet 19 by Milton.md

subnotes/sonnet55.html.title: sonnet55
subnotes/sonnet55.html.description:
Not marble, nor the gilded monuments
Of princes, shall outlive this powerful rhyme;
But you shall shine more bright in these contents
Than unswept stone besmear'd with sluttish time.
When wasteful war shall statues overturn,
And broils root out the work of masonry,
Nor Mars his sword nor war's quick fire shall burn
The living record of your memory.
Gainst death and all-oblivious enmity
Shall you pace forth;your praise shall still find room
Even in the eyes of all posterity
That wear this world out to the ending doom.
So, till the judgment that yourself arise,
You live in this, and dwell in lover's eyes.

subnotes/sonnet60.html.title: sonnet60
subnotes/sonnet60.html.description:
Like as the waves make towards the pebbled shore, (simile)
So do our minutes hasten to their end; (personify);
\ \one life end and another life beginEach changing place with that which goes before,
In sequent toil all forwards do contend.
sequent toil: continuous, laborious effortNativity, once in the main of light,
nativity: a newborn or the state of being bornCrawls to maturity, wherewith being crown'd,
Crooked eclipses 'gainst his glory fight,
And Time that gave doth now his gift confound.
crowned and crooked all means become you climb up and the go down
=>time gives and time take it awayTime doth transfix the flourish set on youth
And delves the parallels in beauty's brow,
Feeds on the rarities of nature's truth,
And nothing stands but for his scythe to mow:
time gradually but steadily devour everythingAnd yet to times in hope, my verse shall stand
Praising thy worth, despite his cruel hand.the same theme: no matter how great, good or beautiful you are, no day all these will fade away

subnotes/story-of-day-labour.html.title: story of day-labour
subnotes/story-of-day-labour.html.description: 1 For the kingdom of heaven is like a landowner who went out early in the morning to hire workers for his vineyard.2 He agreed to pay them a denarius for the day and sent them into his vineyard.3 About nine in the morning he went out and saw others standing in the marketplace doing nothing.4 He told them, “You also go and work in my vineyard, and I will pay you whatever is right.”5 So they went. He went out again about noon and about three in the afternoon and did the same thing.6 About five in the afternoon he went out and found still others standing around. He asked them, “Why have you been standing here all day long doing nothing?”7 “Because no one has hired us,” they answered. He said to them, “You also go and work in my vineyard.”8 When evening came, the owner of the vineyard said to his foreman, “Call the workers and pay them their wages, beginning with the last ones hired and going on to the first.”9 The workers who were hired at about five in the afternoon came and each received a denarius.10 So when those came who were hired first, they expected to receive more. But each one of them also received a denarius.11 When they received it, they began to grumble against the landowner.12 “These who were hired last worked only one hour,” they said, “and you have made them equal to us who have borne the burden of the work and the heat of the day.”13 But he answered one of them, “I am not being unfair to you, friend. Didn’t you agree to work for a denarius?14 Take your pay and go. I want to give the one who was hired last the same as I gave you.15 Don’t I have the right to do what I want with my own money? Or are you envious because I am generous?”16 So the last will be first, and the first will be last.theme: God don't care your quantity of work but your actions, as long as you acted, you will gain the praisedenarius : amount of moneyrecorded in Gospel of Matthew 20
subnotes/story-of-day-labour.html.sourcePath: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/story of day-labour.md

subnotes/story-of-talent.html.title: story of talent
subnotes/story-of-talent.html.description:
back the ancient time, talent used as a measurement of money
and the Lord give his servants talents according to their abilities then the Lord went out to journey
and the one who have five talents gain or earn another five talents
the one who gained two talents do the same thing
but the one who only got a talent who just dig a hole and hide his talentwhen the Lord came back
the one got five talents give back the five talents and the five he earned. Then God praised him good and faithful (because of his diligent and obey god's will) and can enter the joy of thy lord.
the one got two talents also give back all four talents, and god praised him in the same way
but the one who had one talent said the god is powerful does not need his servants do anything. The Lord can do anything. So he hid his talent.
God blame hime wicked and slothful and should be thrown into outer darknesstheme and message
=> We should take care what we be gifted or given(keep it well). We should use the gifts to create more. We have responsibility to use the gifts(to do the second thing).talents : resource, talent etc.
servants : the common peoplerecorded in Gospel of Matthew 25
subnotes/story-of-talent.html.sourcePath: My notes/English/诗歌文化/subnotes/story of talent.md

subnotes/synesthetic-metaphor.html.title: Synesthetic Metaphor
subnotes/synesthetic-metaphor.html.description: Synesthetic metaphor is a figure of speech in which a word or phrase that normally describes one sensory modality (e.g., sight, hearing, touch, taste, smell) is used to describe another, different sensory modality.通感隐喻是一种修辞手法，指将通常用于描述某一种感官范畴（如视觉、听觉、触觉、味觉、嗅觉）的词语或短语，用来描述另一种不同的感官范畴。

Elucidation of the theme (developing; raising problems) Transition to another viewpoint (changing)

subnotes/william-wordsworth.html.title: William Wordsworth
subnotes/william-wordsworth.html.description:
Poetry is spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings; it takes its origin from emotion recollected in tranquility. Poetry is the first and last of all knowledge — it is as immortal as the heart of man. —William Wordsworthone of the greatest poet Romantic poet English Romantic MovementBorn in 1770 in the Lake District of northern England. And received a good education. lost his mother when he was 7 and lost his father at 13 sent off by guardian uncles to grammar school at Hawkshead, moved on in 1787 to St. John's College, Cambridge. spend long time in France, and met a women who is one of his lover. They have a daughter but because of war, Wordsworth had to go back England in 1789 and didn't come back France until 1802 although he already have a daughter with the Frechen women but he married another English women
Composed by the Sea-Side near Calais

英美诗歌与文化.html.title: 英美诗歌与文化
英美诗歌与文化.html.description:
Poetry is spontaneous overflow of powerful feelings; it takes its origin from emtion recollected in tranquility --- William Wordworth A living poem begins with a lump in the throat a homesickeness or a lovesickness. It is a reaching out toward expression, an effort to find fulfillment. A complete poem is one where emtion has found its thought and thought has found the words. ---Robert FrostPoetry delights and instructs. Poetry communicates universal experience. Iambic foot/ Iamb a unit of unstressed and stressed syllables that used in sentece Iambic pentameter which is a form of poetic meter consisting of five pairs of alternating unstressed and stressed syllables. (the red can changed into other types of -meter eg. trimeter) his works are not temporate but immortal, its' contains something that is universal and we can still learn something from it. sonnet 19 by Milton -- story of talent -- story of day-labour

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_minCollapseDepth.description: Only allow outline items to be collapsed if they are at least this many levels deep in the tree.

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_displayTitle.description: Descriptive title to show above the feature

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
